topic: A person accidentally sends a text to the wrong recipient
raw_output:
[story 1]

My thumb hovered over the screen, a clumsy swipe meant for Sarah, but my finger slipped, landing squarely on 'Marcus.' A cold dread washed over me, a sudden, sickening drop in my stomach. I stared at the screen, the words I’d typed—a casual, slightly embarrassing inside joke—now starkly displayed to an unknown entity. My breath hitched, catching in my throat. I yanked my hand back as if burned, my knuckles white against the glass. Panic clawed at my chest, making it hard to draw a full breath. I frantically scrolled through my recent contacts, searching for a way out, a digital lifeline, but every name felt like a trap. The silence in the room suddenly amplified, pressing in on me until I felt physically constricted.

[story 2]

It happened so fast. A misplaced keypress, a momentary lapse in concentration, and the message shot out. I watched the notification bubble appear, and the color drained from my face. My jaw tightened so hard I could feel the strain in my molars. For a long moment, I didn't move; I just sat there, frozen, my eyes fixed on the glowing text. A wave of intense nausea rolled through me, making my vision swim slightly. I brought a trembling hand up to cover my mouth, muffling a sound that was halfway between a gasp and a choked whimper. My heart hammered against my ribs with a frantic, uneven rhythm, a drumbeat of sheer, unadulterated distress. I started shaking, a deep, involuntary tremor running through my limbs.

[story 3]

I reread the message twice. It was supposed to be a joke between friends, a silly thing to send. Now, it was directed at someone I didn't know, someone whose name felt like a brand on my skin. My vision tunneled. The air in the room seemed to thicken, becoming viscous and difficult to pull into my lungs. I stood up abruptly, knocking my chair slightly askew. My hands started to sweat, slick and cold, as I paced three short steps before stopping dead. Every instinct screamed at me to delete it, to undo the catastrophe, but my fingers were locked to the phone, paralyzed by the realization of what I had just unleashed into the world. A low, ragged sound escaped my lips, a sound of profound distress.
